rationale_for_baseline_tier: Although primary dysmenorrhoea is benign, this is acute severe lower abdominal pain with vomiting and inability to keep fluids down in a reproductive-age woman whose pregnancy status is unconfirmed — a pregnancy test (to exclude ectopic) and in-person assessment cannot wait and cannot be done via chat, so an urgent same-day encounter is required (T3). Ectopic/surgical emergencies carry a hard ED/000 (T4/T5) safety-net; failed rehydration routes to ED.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sharp/constant pain on one side, feeling faint or collapsing, shoulder-tip pain, or abnormal (not typical period) bleeding — especially if pregnancy is not excluded
escalation_edges.rationale: Possible ruptured ectopic pregnancy — a life-threatening emergency; pregnancy must be excluded.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the pain becomes sharp and constant on one side, you feel faint or collapse, get shoulder-tip pain, or have abnormal bleeding — call 000 or go to ED immediately, especially as we haven't confirmed you're not pregnant.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Migratory right-lower-quadrant pain, fever, offensive vaginal discharge, or increasing localised peritoneal pain
escalation_edges.rationale: Appendicitis, ovarian torsion or PID needing urgent surgical/gynae assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the pain moves to or becomes sharp on the right lower side, or you develop a fever or offensive discharge — go to the Emergency Department that day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Still unable to keep any fluids down after the antiemetic, not passing urine, or marked faintness

contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower threshold for ED given the pregnancy question and dehydration; ensure a same-day pathway.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This looks like severe period pain, but because you could be pregnant we must do a pregnancy test today and have you assessed in person — that can't be done over chat. In the meantime, the anti-nausea medicine and an anti-inflammatory should help, with small sips of fluid.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Urgent same-day pregnancy test + in-person assessment; 000/ED for ectopic/surgical or dehydration features
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000/ED for one-sided pain, faintness, shoulder-tip pain, or if you can't keep fluids down.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Presumptive primary dysmenorrhoea in a reproductive-age woman with vomiting/dehydration; pregnancy NOT yet excluded — urgent beta-hCG and assessment required; appendicitis/torsion/PID screened negative; NSAID + antiemetic + rehydration; emergency + dehydration safety-nets given.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Reassuring 'just period pain' and treating without arranging a pregnancy test / excluding ectopic.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: A ruptured ectopic pregnancy is mislabelled as period pain and the patient collapses from haemorrhage.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Calling an ambulance for settling period pain with a negative pregnancy test and stable observations — though a same-day in-person pathway is required.
